name: Kazakhstan
tagline: From the Saka horsemen of the Great Steppe to the world's largest landlocked nation.
summary: Kazakhstan occupies the heart of the Eurasian steppe, a vast grassland that for three millennia nourished mounted nomads, from the Iron Age Saka (the eastern Scythians) through the Turkic khaganates and the Silk Road towns of Otrar, Taraz, and Turkestan. Genghis Khan's armies stormed the region in 1219-1221 and folded it into the Mongol empire's western wing, the Golden Horde; out of the Horde's breakup the sultans Janibek and Kerei founded the Kazakh Khanate around 1465, an event Kazakhs regard as the root of their statehood, organised into three jüz (hordes). Russia gradually absorbed the steppe from the 1730s, and after 1917 it became the Kazakh Soviet Socialist Republic, scene of the catastrophic 1930-33 collectivisation famine, the Virgin Lands campaign, the Gulag, and the Semipalatinsk nuclear test site. Kazakhstan was the last Soviet republic to declare independence, on 16 December 1991, and under Nursultan Nazarbayev built a new capital at Astana and a resource-driven economy before a turbulent succession to Kassym-Jomart Tokayev amid the January 2022 unrest.

founding.label: Foundation of the Kazakh Khanate by Janibek and Kerei
founding.detail: Kazakh statehood is conventionally traced to about 1465, when the sultans Janibek and Kerei, descendants of Genghis Khan through Urus Khan of the Jochid line, broke with the Uzbek ruler Abu'l-Khayr Khan and led their followers into Zhetysu (Semirechye, the 'land of seven rivers') in the southeast of present-day Kazakhstan, where they established an independent realm. The Kazakh Khanate that grew from this migration is regarded as the political and ethnic root of the Kazakh nation; its 550th anniversary was marked nationally in 2015. The khanate later organised itself into three jüz, or hordes, the Great, Middle, and Little, and endured until the Russian Empire abolished the last of its khans in the nineteenth century. The modern Republic of Kazakhstan declared its independence on 16 December 1991, the last of the Soviet republics to do so.

quickFacts.label: Steppe statehood
quickFacts.value: Kazakh Khanate founded c. 1465 by Janibek and Kerei

quickFacts.label: Russian conquest
quickFacts.value: Hordes accept Russian protection from 1731; khanates abolished 1822-1848

quickFacts.label: Soviet era
quickFacts.value: Kazakh A.S.S.R. (1925); union republic from 5 December 1936

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: 16 December 1991, the last Soviet republic to declare

eras.title: The Saka and the Turkic Steppe
eras.standfirst: Iron Age horse-warriors, then Turkic khaganates and Silk Road towns, made the Kazakh steppe a crossroads of Eurasia.
body: Long before there were Kazakhs, the grasslands of present-day Kazakhstan belonged to mounted nomads. In the first millennium BCE the region was home to the Saka, the eastern branch of the Scythian world: Iranian-speaking horse-warriors whose elite were buried in great burial mounds (kurgans) with lavish gold. The most famous, the 'Golden Man' of the Issyk kurgan near Almaty, was interred around the fourth century BCE in a suit of thousands of gold plaques. Greek, Persian, and Chinese writers knew these peoples as fierce riders and archers who herded sheep and horses across the open steppe between the Caspian and the Altai.

From the sixth century CE the steppe was dominated by Turkic-speaking confederations. The Türk (Göktürk) Khaganate, a nomadic empire reaching from Manchuria to the Black Sea, split into eastern and western halves, and Zhetysu (Semirechye) in the southeast became a heartland of the Western Turkic Khaganate. Successive Turkic states, the Türgesh, the Qarluqs, and from the tenth century the Muslim Karakhanids, ruled the south, while Islam spread among the settled population. These centuries fixed the Turkic language and, gradually, the Islamic faith that would define the later Kazakhs.

Across southern Kazakhstan ran a vital artery of the Silk Road. Caravans moving between China and the Mediterranean passed through a chain of oasis towns, Sayram (Ispijab), Otrar, Taraz, Sauran, and Yasi (later Turkestan), which grew rich as markets and as centres of learning, craft, and Islamic scholarship. Otrar, near the Syr Darya, was among the wealthiest, and Taraz was celebrated as a 'city of merchants.' These towns linked the nomads of the steppe to the wider Muslim and Chinese worlds and made the region a true crossroads of medieval Eurasia, until the Mongol storm of the thirteenth century.

events.title: The Saka 'Golden Man' of Issyk
events.summary: A high-status Saka warrior is buried in the Issyk kurgan near Almaty in a costume of thousands of gold plaques, a celebrated emblem of the Iron Age nomad cultures that dominated the Kazakh steppe in the first millennium BCE.

events.title: Turkic khaganates dominate the steppe
events.summary: Turkic-speaking confederations, beginning with the Türk (Göktürk) Khaganate and later the Karakhanids, take control of the region; Zhetysu becomes a heartland of the Western Turkic realm, fixing the Turkic language and spreading Islam.

events.title: Silk Road towns of the south flourish
events.summary: Caravan cities such as Otrar, Taraz, Sauran, and Yasi prosper on the trade between China and the Mediterranean, becoming centres of commerce, craft, and Islamic learning along the southern Kazakh steppe.

pullquote.text: The Saka elite were buried in kurgans with gold by the thousand-plate; the Issyk 'Golden Man' remains a national emblem of the steppe.

eras.title: The Mongol Conquest and the Golden Horde
eras.standfirst: Genghis Khan razed the Silk Road cities and bound the steppe to the Mongol empire's western wing, the Golden Horde.
body: In 1219 the governor of Otrar seized and killed a Mongol trade caravan, an act Genghis Khan treated as a mortal insult. The Mongol army descended on the Syr Darya towns, besieging Otrar for roughly five months before sacking it, then sweeping through the wealthy realm of Khwarezm in a campaign of 1219-1221 that devastated the settled south. The conquest shattered the Silk Road cities of the region and incorporated the steppe into the vast empire Genghis Khan was assembling across Eurasia, ending the era of independent Turkic and Karakhanid rule in Kazakhstan.

After Genghis Khan's death his empire was divided among his sons. The lands of the western steppe, including most of present-day Kazakhstan, fell to the line of his eldest son, Jochi, and crystallised into the khanate the Russians later called the Golden Horde, the empire's western wing, which flourished from the mid-thirteenth to the late fourteenth century. Its population was a blend of Mongols and the far more numerous Turks; the Turkic language soon prevailed and Islam became the dominant faith, deepening the Turkic-Muslim character of the steppe peoples.

As the Golden Horde weakened in the fifteenth century it fragmented into rival successor khanates. From the eastern Qipchaq steppe emerged the nomadic Uzbek confederation under Abu'l-Khayr Khan, and it was a breakaway from his rule, led by the Jochid sultans Janibek and Kerei, that produced the Kazakhs. It was also in this period that the steppe's most celebrated monument rose: at Yasi (Turkestan), Timur (Tamerlane) ordered between 1389 and 1405 the great Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi over the tomb of the revered twelfth-century Sufi master, a masterpiece of Timurid architecture left unfinished at Timur's death.

events.title: Mongol conquest of the Syr Darya and Khwarezm
events.summary: Provoked by the killing of his envoys at Otrar, Genghis Khan storms the Silk Road towns and overruns Khwarezm; Otrar falls after a months-long siege, and the steppe is absorbed into the Mongol empire.

events.title: The Golden Horde rules the western steppe
events.summary: The lands of present-day Kazakhstan pass to the line of Jochi, forming the western wing of the Mongol empire known as the Golden Horde, where Turks outnumber Mongols and Islam becomes the prevailing faith.

events.title: Timur builds the Mausoleum of Khoja Ahmed Yasawi
events.summary: At Yasi (Turkestan) Timur orders a monumental mausoleum over the tomb of the Sufi master Khoja Ahmed Yasawi; left unfinished at Timur's death in 1405, it became a masterpiece of Timurid architecture and a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2003.

pullquote.text: In this khanate the Mongols were greatly outnumbered by Turks; the Turkish language soon displaced Mongol, and Islam became the prevailing religion.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopædia Britannica, on the Golden Horde

eras.title: The Kazakh Khanate and the Three Jüz
eras.standfirst: Janibek and Kerei founded the Kazakh state on the steppe; it grew into three jüz and faced a deadly Dzungar onslaught.
body: Around 1465 the sultans Janibek and Kerei, descendants of Genghis Khan, led a body of nomads away from the Uzbek khan Abu'l-Khayr and into Zhetysu, in the southeast of present-day Kazakhstan, where they founded an independent realm. The people who followed them came to be called Kazakhs, and the Kazakh Khanate they established is regarded by Kazakhs as the foundation of their statehood, the moment a distinct Kazakh nation entered history. Over the following century the khanate expanded across the steppe, absorbing Turkic clans and asserting control over the old caravan towns of the Syr Darya.

The Kazakhs organised themselves into three great tribal divisions known as jüz, or hordes: the Great Horde (Uly Jüz) in the southeast and Zhetysu, the Middle Horde (Orta Jüz) in the central and northern steppe, and the Little Horde (Kishi Jüz) in the west toward the Caspian and Ural. Each jüz comprised a confederation of tribes with its own pastures and leaders, while khans claiming descent from Genghis Khan provided an overarching, if often loose, political unity. This three-fold structure, bound by a shared language, Islam, and genealogy, became the enduring framework of Kazakh society.

From the late seventeenth century the Kazakhs faced a mortal threat from the east: the Dzungars, a powerful Mongol Buddhist confederation. Repeated Dzungar invasions culminated in the catastrophe Kazakhs remember as the 'Great Disaster' (Aktaban Shubyryndy) around 1723, when whole communities were driven from their pastures with heavy loss of life. The wars exhausted the khanate and shattered its cohesion, and it was this weakness, the need for a powerful protector against the Dzungars, that opened the door to the next great force on the steppe: the expanding Russian Empire.

events.title: Foundation of the Kazakh Khanate
events.summary: The Jochid sultans Janibek and Kerei break with Abu'l-Khayr Khan and lead their followers into Zhetysu, founding the Kazakh Khanate, the realm Kazakhs regard as the root of their nation and statehood.

events.title: The three jüz take shape
events.summary: Kazakh society organises into three hordes, the Great, Middle, and Little jüz, each a confederation of tribes with its own pastures, united by language, Islam, and descent and headed by Genghisid khans.

events.title: The 'Great Disaster' of the Dzungar wars
events.summary: Devastating invasions by the Buddhist Dzungar confederation drive Kazakhs from their pastures with great loss of life, an episode remembered as the Aktaban Shubyryndy that gravely weakens the khanate.

figures.name: Janibek and Kerei
figures.role: Founding khans of the Kazakh Khanate
figures.blurb: Two Jochid sultans, descendants of Genghis Khan through Urus Khan, who around 1465 led their followers away from Uzbek rule into Zhetysu and founded the Kazakh Khanate, regarded as the origin of Kazakh statehood.

pullquote.text: The Kazakh Khanate was formed around 1465 by Kerei Khan and Janibek Khan, the foundation of Kazakh statehood.

eras.title: The Russian Conquest of the Steppe
eras.standfirst: Seeking shelter from the Dzungars, the hordes accepted Russian protection; over a century the empire abolished the khans and colonised the steppe.
body: Pressed by the Dzungars, the khans of the three jüz turned to Russia for protection. In 1731 Abu'l-Khayr Khan of the Little Horde swore allegiance to the Russian Empire, followed by the Middle Horde in 1740 and part of the Great Horde in 1742. What the Kazakhs intended as a defensive alliance, Russia treated as the first step toward annexation. The great khan Ablai (Abylai), elected ruler of the Kazakhs in 1771, manoeuvred between the Russian and Chinese (Qing) empires to preserve a measure of independence, and remains a national hero, but the long-term tide ran toward Russian control.

Through the nineteenth century Russia tightened its grip, building lines of forts across the steppe and encouraging Slavic settlement on Kazakh grazing land. To break Kazakh autonomy, the empire abolished the khanates one by one: the Middle Horde in 1822, the Little Horde in 1824, and the Great Horde in 1848. Kazakh resistance was fierce and repeated. The most formidable revolt was led by Kenesary Kasymov, a grandson of Ablai Khan, who fought the Russians across the steppe from 1837 until his death in 1847 in a bid to restore the independent khanate.

Russian rule transformed the steppe. The seizure of pasture for Slavic colonists strained the nomadic economy, while contact with Russian and European ideas produced a new Kazakh intelligentsia. Its towering figure was the poet and thinker Abai (Abay) Kunanbaiuly (1845-1904), an aristocrat who blended Kazakh oral tradition with Russian literature, translating Pushkin and Lermontov and laying the foundation of the modern Kazakh literary language. By 1917, on the eve of revolution, Kazakhstan was a colonised borderland of a Russian Empire about to collapse into war and revolution.

events.title: The Little Horde accepts Russian protection
events.summary: Abu'l-Khayr Khan of the Little Horde swears allegiance to the Russian Empire for protection against the Dzungars; the Middle Horde follows in 1740 and part of the Great Horde in 1742, opening the steppe to Russian power.

events.title: Ablai Khan elected ruler of the Kazakhs
events.summary: Ablai (Abylai) is elected khan of the Kazakhs and balances between the Russian and Qing empires to preserve a degree of independence; he endures as a national symbol of Kazakh sovereignty.

events.title: Russia abolishes the Kazakh khanates
events.summary: To end Kazakh autonomy, Russia abolishes the khanate of the Middle Horde in 1822, the Little Horde in 1824, and the Great Horde in 1848, dismantling the political structure of the Kazakh Khanate.

events.title: Kenesary Kasymov's revolt
events.summary: Kenesary Kasymov, a grandson of Ablai Khan, leads the largest Kazakh uprising against Russian rule, fighting to restore the independent khanate until he is killed in 1847.

figures.name: Ablai Khan (Abylai)
figures.blurb: Elected ruler of the Kazakhs in 1771, Ablai Khan defended the steppe against the Dzungars and manoeuvred between the Russian and Qing empires to preserve Kazakh independence; he is revered as a unifier and national hero.

figures.name: Abai Kunanbaiuly (Abay Kunanbayev)
figures.blurb: The founder of modern written Kazakh literature, Abai fused Kazakh oral verse with Russian models, translating Pushkin and Lermontov and laying the basis of the modern Kazakh literary language and national thought.

pullquote.text: Abai laid the basis with his verse for the development of the modern Kazakh literary language and its poetry.

eras.title: Soviet Kazakhstan
eras.standfirst: Collectivisation brought a famine that killed a third of the Kazakhs; later came the Virgin Lands, the Gulag, and the nuclear steppe.
body: After the 1917 revolution and the Russian Civil War, the Bolsheviks fastened Soviet control on the steppe. An autonomous republic created in 1920 was renamed the Kazak A.S.S.R. in 1925, and Kazakhstan became a full union republic of the USSR on 5 December 1936. From the late 1920s the Soviet state forced the nomadic Kazakhs to settle and collectivised their herds. The result was the deadliest catastrophe in Kazakh history: the famine of 1930-33 (Asharshylyk), driven by collectivisation and ruthless requisition under the republic's leader Filipp Goloshchekin, which killed an estimated 1.5 million people, on the order of a third or more of all Kazakhs, the highest proportional toll of any people in the Soviet famines. The figures are estimates, but scholars agree the losses were vast.

Stalinist Kazakhstan also became a vast prison and dumping ground. The steppe held some of the largest camps of the Gulag, including Karlag near Karaganda and the Alzhir camp for the wives of 'enemies of the people,' while entire nationalities, Koreans, Volga Germans, Chechens, and others, were deported to Kazakhstan in the 1930s and 1940s. After 1953 Nikita Khrushchev's Virgin Lands campaign ploughed up millions of hectares of northern Kazakh steppe for grain, drawing in hundreds of thousands of mostly Slavic settlers and shifting the republic's demography so far that Kazakhs became a minority in their own land for a generation.

The steppe paid a further, hidden price for Soviet power. From 1949 the Semipalatinsk Test Site in the northeast served as the principal proving ground for Soviet nuclear weapons; some 456 nuclear explosions were carried out there until 1989, exposing surrounding communities to fallout with lasting effects on health that are still being measured. By the late 1980s discontent was surfacing: anti-Russian protests erupted in Almaty in December 1986 (the Jeltoqsan events) after Moscow installed a non-Kazakh party boss, and a popular anti-nuclear movement, Nevada-Semipalatinsk, helped force the closure of the test site in 1991, the year the Soviet Union itself dissolved.

events.title: The Kazakh famine (Asharshylyk)
events.summary: Forced collectivisation and the settling of nomads under Filipp Goloshchekin produce a man-made famine that kills an estimated 1.5 million people, around a third of all Kazakhs, the deadliest catastrophe in Kazakh history; the toll is an estimate.

events.title: Kazakh S.S.R. becomes a union republic
events.summary: Kazakhstan, an autonomous republic since 1920 and renamed the Kazak A.S.S.R. in 1925, is raised to a full constituent republic of the Soviet Union under the 1936 constitution.

events.title: Semipalatinsk nuclear test site
events.summary: The Soviet Union conducts some 456 nuclear tests at the Semipalatinsk site in northeastern Kazakhstan between 1949 and 1989, exposing local populations to radioactive fallout with severe long-term health effects.

events.title: Khrushchev's Virgin Lands campaign
events.summary: Nikita Khrushchev's drive to plough up the northern Kazakh steppe for grain brings hundreds of thousands of mostly Slavic settlers, transforming the republic's demography so that Kazakhs become a minority in Kazakhstan.

figures.name: Filipp Goloshchekin
figures.role: First Secretary of the Kazak A.S.S.R.
figures.blurb: The Soviet party leader who imposed forced collectivisation and the settling of nomads in Kazakhstan, policies that produced the catastrophic famine of 1930-33; he was himself executed in 1941 during the Stalinist purges.

pullquote.text: Between roughly a third and a half of all Kazakhs perished in the famine of 1930-33, the highest proportional toll of any people in the Soviet famines.

eras.title: Independent Kazakhstan
eras.standfirst: The last republic to leave the USSR became an oil-rich state under Nazarbayev, a new capital at Astana, and a turbulent transition to Tokayev.
body: Kazakhstan declared its independence on 16 December 1991, the last of the fifteen Soviet republics to do so; days later the USSR ceased to exist. Its first president, Nursultan Nazarbayev, the republic's Communist Party leader, would dominate the new state for nearly three decades. Independent Kazakhstan voluntarily renounced the large Soviet nuclear arsenal it had inherited, closing the Semipalatinsk test site and transferring the warheads to Russia, a decision that won it international goodwill. In 1997 Nazarbayev moved the capital from Almaty in the southeast to Akmola on the northern steppe, renamed Astana ('capital') in 1998 and rebuilt as a showcase city of modern architecture.

Nazarbayev's long rule rested on the country's immense natural wealth, vast reserves of oil, gas, uranium, and metals that made Kazakhstan the largest economy in Central Asia, alongside a tightly controlled political system with little real opposition. The state pursued a 'multi-vector' foreign policy, balancing Russia, China, and the West. In 2019 Nazarbayev stepped down as president, handing power to his chosen successor, Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, while retaining sweeping influence as head of the Security Council and 'Leader of the Nation'; the capital was briefly renamed Nur-Sultan in his honour.

In January 2022 that arrangement broke apart. Protests that began on 2 January over a sharp rise in fuel prices in the western oil town of Zhanaozen spread nationwide and turned violent, especially in Almaty, in the unrest Kazakhs call Qandy Qantar ('Bloody January'). President Tokayev declared a state of emergency, called in troops from the Russian-led CSTO alliance, and authorised the use of lethal force; by official counts about 227 people died. Tokayev used the crisis to sideline Nazarbayev's allies and consolidate his own authority, the capital reverted to Astana, and he won a fresh seven-year term later in 2022, leaving Kazakhstan, in 2026, a resource-rich state still navigating a fraught transition from its founding leader's long shadow.

events.title: Kazakhstan declares independence
events.summary: Kazakhstan proclaims itself a sovereign, independent state, the last of the Soviet republics to declare independence, with Nursultan Nazarbayev as president; the USSR formally dissolves days later.

events.title: Capital moved to Astana
events.summary: Nazarbayev relocates the capital from Almaty to Akmola on the northern steppe; renamed Astana ('capital') in 1998, it is rebuilt as a planned showcase city of modern architecture.

events.title: Nazarbayev resigns; Tokayev succeeds
events.summary: After nearly three decades in power, Nazarbayev steps down as president, handing office to Kassym-Jomart Tokayev while retaining major influence; the capital is briefly renamed Nur-Sultan in his honour.

events.title: 'Bloody January' unrest
events.summary: Protests over fuel prices spread nationwide and turn violent; Tokayev declares a state of emergency, summons CSTO troops, and authorises lethal force, with about 227 deaths reported, then sidelines Nazarbayev's allies to consolidate power.

figures.name: Nursultan Nazarbayev
figures.blurb: Soviet-era party chief who led independent Kazakhstan from 1991 to 2019, renounced its nuclear arsenal, moved the capital to Astana, and built an oil-rich but tightly controlled state, retaining influence as 'Leader of the Nation' until the 2022 unrest.

figures.name: Kassym-Jomart Tokayev
figures.blurb: Career diplomat who succeeded Nazarbayev in 2019 and, after the violent 'Bloody January' of 2022, used the crisis to break his predecessor's network and consolidate power, restoring the capital's name to Astana and winning a new term that year.

pullquote.text: On 16 December 1991 Kazakhstan became the last of the Soviet republics to proclaim its independence.

figures.name: Ablai Khan (Abylai)
figures.blurb: Elected ruler of the Kazakhs in 1771, who defended the steppe against the Dzungars and balanced the Russian and Qing empires to preserve Kazakh independence; a revered national hero and unifier.

figures.name: Abai Kunanbaiuly (Abay Kunanbayev)
figures.blurb: Founder of modern written Kazakh literature, who fused Kazakh oral verse with Russian models and laid the basis of the modern Kazakh literary language and national thought.

figures.name: Nursultan Nazarbayev
figures.blurb: Led independent Kazakhstan from 1991 to 2019, renounced its nuclear arsenal, founded the new capital Astana, and built a resource-rich but tightly controlled state.
